title: Lamb Ouzi Whole Slow Roasted

seo.title: Lamb Ouzi Recipe: Whole Slow Roasted Middle Eastern Lamb
seo.metaDescription: Master the art of Middle Eastern Lamb Ouzi! Our whole slow-roasted lamb recipe is authentic, tender, and perfect for a show-stopping dinner. Get the full recipe here!
lowCompetitionKeywords: slow roasted lamb ouzi at home
seo.h1: Authentic Lamb Ouzi: Whole Slow Roasted Middle Eastern Delight
h2Headings: Ingredients for Whole Slow Roasted Lamb Ouzi
Step-by-Step Guide to Making Lamb Ouzi
Tips for Perfectly Tender Slow Roasted Lamb
Frequently Asked Questions about Lamb Ouzi
seo.imageAlt: A magnificent whole slow roasted Lamb Ouzi dish, garnished with nuts and herbs, ready to be served.
seo.imageCaption: Our show-stopping whole slow-roasted Lamb Ouzi, a centerpiece for any Middle Eastern feast.

description: Experience the grandeur of Middle Eastern cuisine with our authentic Lamb Ouzi. This show-stopping dish features a whole slow-roasted lamb leg, incredibly tender and infused with aromatic spices, served traditionally over fragrant spiced rice. Perfect for festive gatherings, it promises a culinary journey of rich flavors and unforgettable textures.

instructions.title: Prepare and Marinate the Lamb
instructions.text: Pat the lamb leg thoroughly dry with paper towels. In a small bowl, combine the olive oil, 1 tablespoon of seven-spice blend, 1 teaspoon of ground cinnamon, 1 teaspoon of ground cardamom, 1/2 teaspoon of ground nutmeg, 1 teaspoon of black pepper, 2 teaspoons of salt, minced garlic, and lemon juice to create a fragrant paste. Generously rub this paste all over the lamb leg, ensuring it penetrates any crevices. Cover the lamb and refrigerate for at least 2 hours, or for best flavor, preferably overnight.

instructions.title: Slow Roast the Lamb
instructions.text: Preheat your oven to 160°C (325°F). Place the marinated lamb leg in a large roasting pan. Pour 1-2 cups of water or broth into the bottom of the pan to keep the lamb moist during cooking. Cover the pan tightly with aluminum foil to trap the steam. Roast for 3 to 4 hours, or until the lamb is incredibly fork-tender. For the last 30-45 minutes, remove the foil to allow the lamb to develop a beautiful golden-brown crust. The internal temperature should reach 63°C (145°F) for medium-rare or 71°C (160°F) for medium.

instructions.title: Prepare the Ouzi Rice Base
instructions.text: While the lamb roasts, begin preparing the Ouzi rice. Melt the ghee or unsalted butter in a large, heavy-bottomed pot over medium heat. Add the finely chopped onion and sauté until it becomes softened and translucent, which typically takes about 5-7 minutes. Add the ground lamb or beef to the pot and cook, breaking it up with a spoon, until it is fully browned. Drain any excess fat from the pot.

instructions.title: Season and Add Vegetables
instructions.text: Stir in the remaining 1 tablespoon of seven-spice blend, 1 teaspoon of ground allspice, 1 teaspoon of ground cinnamon, 1 teaspoon of salt, and 1/2 teaspoon of black pepper with the ground meat and onions. Cook for another 2 minutes, stirring constantly, until the spices are fragrant. Add the frozen peas and diced carrots to the mixture, cooking for an additional 3-5 minutes until they are slightly tender.

instructions.title: Cook the Fragrant Rice
instructions.text: Drain the soaked basmati rice thoroughly and add it to the pot with the meat and vegetable mixture. Stir gently to combine all the ingredients without mashing the rice. Pour in the 4 cups of lamb or chicken broth. Bring the liquid to a rolling boil, then immediately reduce the heat to very low. Cover the pot tightly with a lid and simmer for 15-20 minutes, or until all the liquid has been absorbed and the rice is tender. It is crucial not to lift the lid during this cooking process. Once cooked, remove the pot from the heat and let it rest, still covered, for 10 minutes. Finally, fluff the rice gently with a fork.

instructions.title: Assemble and Serve Your Ouzi Feast
instructions.text: Once the slow-roasted lamb leg is perfectly cooked and has rested for at least 15 minutes (allowing the juices to redistribute), carve it into generous portions. To serve, spoon a large, fragrant bed of the Ouzi rice onto a magnificent serving platter. Arrange the carved lamb pieces over the rice. Garnish generously with the toasted pine nuts, toasted slivered almonds, and fresh chopped parsley. Serve immediately and delight in this truly magnificent Middle Eastern feast.

tips: For the most tender lamb, marinate it overnight. This allows the spices and lemon juice to deeply penetrate the meat, enhancing flavor and tenderness.
Do not overcrowd your roasting pan. If roasting multiple lamb legs, use separate pans to ensure even cooking and browning.

After removing the lamb from the oven, let it rest, covered with foil, for at least 15-20 minutes before carving. This allows the juices to redistribute, resulting in a more tender and flavorful meat.
article:
Embark on a culinary journey to the heart of the Middle East with a dish that embodies celebration, hospitality, and exquisite flavor: Lamb Ouzi. This magnificent Middle Eastern dinner recipe, often featuring a whole slow-roasted lamb, is more than just a meal; it's an experience. Perfect for grand gatherings, festive occasions, or simply a luxurious family dinner, Lamb Ouzi promises melt-in-your-mouth tenderness, aromatic spices, and a feast for all the senses. Preparing it is a labor of love, but the reward of a perfectly cooked, fall-off-the-bone lamb, infused with rich flavors and served with fragrant spiced rice, is absolutely unparalleled. Get ready to transform your kitchen into a haven of Middle Eastern aromas and create a truly unforgettable dining event.

## What is Lamb Ouzi?

Lamb Ouzi, sometimes spelled Oozie or Ghuzi, is a quintessential Middle Eastern celebratory dish. At its core, it involves slow-roasted lamb or goat, often a substantial cut like a leg or shoulder, served over a bed of highly aromatic, spiced rice. While the term "whole" can sometimes refer to an entire lamb carcass roasted for truly massive feasts, for home cooks and most restaurant settings, it typically signifies a large, bone-in cut of lamb that forms the impressive centerpiece. This dish is synonymous with major holidays like Eid, weddings, and large family gatherings across the Levant and Gulf regions, including countries like Syria, Jordan, Lebanon, and Iraq. The magic of Lamb Ouzi lies in the harmonious blend of tender, succulent meat, usually seasoned with a complex mix of Middle Eastern spices, and the rich, flavorful rice studded with nuts, peas, and sometimes dried fruits. It’s a dish designed to impress, delight, and bring people together around a shared table.

## Ingredients for Whole Slow Roasted Lamb Ouzi

Crafting an authentic and delectable Lamb Ouzi requires a thoughtful selection of ingredients. While the "whole" lamb might be a leg or shoulder for practicality, the principles of flavor and tenderness remain.

For the Lamb:
*   **One large bone-in lamb leg or shoulder (4-6 lbs / 2-3 kg):** Bone-in is crucial for flavor and moisture retention during slow roasting.
*   **Olive oil:** For rubbing and helping spices adhere.
*   **Garlic:** 6-8 cloves, minced or crushed.
*   **Onion:** 1 large, roughly chopped (for the roasting pan).
*   **Middle Eastern Seven Spice blend:** 2-3 tablespoons (a mix of allspice, black pepper, cinnamon, cloves, nutmeg, cardamom, and ginger). If unavailable, you can substitute with individual spices.
*   **Ground cumin:** 1 tablespoon.
*   **Ground coriander:** 1 tablespoon.
*   **Smoked paprika:** 1 teaspoon (optional, for depth of color and flavor).
*   **Salt:** 2 teaspoons, or to taste.
*   **Black pepper:** 1 teaspoon, freshly ground.
*   **Water or chicken/beef broth:** 2-3 cups, for the roasting pan.

For the Spiced Rice (Ouzi Rice):
*   **Basmati rice:** 2 cups, rinsed thoroughly.
*   **Olive oil or ghee:** 2 tablespoons.
*   **Onion:** 1 medium, finely chopped.
*   **Garlic:** 2 cloves, minced.
*   **Middle Eastern Seven Spice blend:** 1-2 teaspoons.
*   **Ground cinnamon:** 1/2 teaspoon.
*   **Ground cardamom:** 1/4 teaspoon.
*   **Chicken or vegetable broth:** 3-4 cups (or water + bouillon).
*   **Frozen peas:** 1 cup.
*   **Carrots:** 1/2 cup, finely diced (optional).
*   **Toasted slivered almonds or pine nuts:** 1/2 cup, for garnish and mixing in.
*   **Raisins or dried cranberries:** 1/4 cup (optional, for sweetness).
*   **Salt and pepper:** To taste.

For Garnish:
*   Fresh parsley, chopped.
*   Extra toasted nuts.

## Step-by-Step Guide to Making Lamb Ouzi

Creating this impressive Lamb Ouzi dish is a process, but each step contributes to its magnificent outcome.

1.  **Prepare the Lamb:** Pat the lamb leg dry with paper towels. Using a sharp knife, make several deep incisions all over the surface of the lamb, about 1-2 inches deep.
2.  **Make the Spice Rub:** In a small bowl, combine the olive oil, minced garlic, Seven Spice blend, cumin, coriander, smoked paprika (if using), salt, and black pepper. Mix well to form a paste.
3.  **Marinate:** Generously rub the spice paste all over the lamb, ensuring it gets into all the incisions. Place the lamb in a large roasting pan, cover tightly with plastic wrap, and refrigerate for at least 6 hours, ideally overnight, to allow the flavors to penetrate deeply.
4.  **Slow Roasting:** Preheat your oven to a low temperature, around 300°F (150°C). Remove the lamb from the refrigerator and let it sit at room temperature for about an hour before roasting. Place the chopped onion in the bottom of the roasting pan around the lamb. Pour 2-3 cups of water or broth into the pan. Cover the roasting pan tightly with heavy-duty aluminum foil or a lid.
5.  **Roast for Hours:** Place the covered roasting pan in the preheated oven. Roast for 5-7 hours, or until the lamb is incredibly tender and easily pulls away from the bone. The exact time will depend on the size of your lamb cut. Halfway through, you can baste the lamb with the pan juices.
6.  **Brown the Lamb:** For the last 30-60 minutes of cooking, remove the foil or lid. Increase the oven temperature to 375°F (190°C) and continue roasting until the lamb skin is beautifully browned and slightly crispy.
7.  **Rest the Lamb:** Once cooked, remove the lamb from the oven, transfer it to a large cutting board, and tent it loosely with foil. Let it rest for at least 20-30 minutes before carving. This step is crucial for juicy, tender meat.
8.  **Prepare the Spiced Rice:** While the lamb is resting, prepare the Ouzi rice. Heat olive oil or ghee in a large pot or Dutch oven over medium heat. Add the chopped onion and sauté until softened and translucent, about 5-7 minutes. Add the minced garlic, Seven Spice blend, cinnamon, and cardamom, and cook for another minute until fragrant.
9.  **Cook the Rice:** Add the rinsed basmati rice to the pot and stir to coat it with the spices and oil. Pour in the broth, season with salt and pepper, and bring to a boil. Reduce heat to low, cover tightly, and simmer for 15-18 minutes, or until the liquid is absorbed and the rice is tender.
10. **Finish the Rice:** Remove the pot from the heat and let it sit, covered, for 5-10 minutes. Fluff the rice with a fork, then gently stir in the frozen peas, diced carrots (if using), half of the toasted nuts, and raisins (if using).

## Tips for Perfectly Tender Slow Roasted Lamb

Achieving that coveted fall-off-the-bone tenderness for your Lamb Ouzi is simpler than you might think, provided you follow a few key principles:

*   **Marination is Key:** Don't skimp on the marinating time. Allowing the lamb to soak in the spice rub for at least 6 hours, or preferably overnight, allows the flavors to deeply penetrate the meat and helps to tenderize it. The acidity from any optional yogurt or lemon in the marinade also aids in breaking down tough fibers.
*   **Low and Slow is the Way to Go:** This is the golden rule for tender roasted lamb. Cooking at a low temperature (around 300°F / 150°C) for an extended period ensures that the connective tissues in the lamb slowly break down, resulting in incredibly succulent and tender meat, rather than tough and dry.
*   **Maintain Moisture:** Covering the roasting pan tightly with foil or a lid during the initial long roast traps steam, creating a moist environment that prevents the lamb from drying out. Adding liquid (water or broth) to the bottom of the pan further contributes to this. Basting occasionally with the pan juices also helps.
*   **Rest, Rest, Rest:** Once the lamb is out of the oven, it's vital to let it rest, tented with foil, for at least 20-30 minutes. This allows the juices within the meat to redistribute, ensuring every slice is moist and flavorful. Cutting into it too soon will cause the juices to run out, leaving you with drier meat.
*   **Bone-In for Flavor:** Opting for a bone-in leg or shoulder of lamb adds a significant depth of flavor to the meat and the pan juices. The bone also helps in even cooking and moisture retention.
*   **Trust Your Thermometer:** While visual cues are helpful, an internal meat thermometer is your best friend for precision. For fall-off-the-bone tenderness, the lamb should reach an internal temperature upwards of 200°F (93°C) in the thickest part.

## Serving Suggestions for Lamb Ouzi

Lamb Ouzi is a complete and satisfying meal on its own, with the spiced rice forming the perfect bed for the succulent lamb. However, you can enhance the experience with a few complementary sides:

*   **Fresh Salads:** A vibrant Fattoush or Tabbouleh salad provides a refreshing counterpoint to the richness of the lamb.
*   **Yogurt Sauce:** A simple, plain yogurt or a cooling labneh (strained yogurt) can be offered on the side, often drizzled with a little olive oil and a sprinkle of dried mint.
*   **Warm Pita Bread:** Perfect for scooping up any extra rice or tender bits of lamb.
*   **Pickles:** Assorted Middle Eastern pickles (torshi) add a tangy crunch that cuts through the richness.

## Variations and Pairings

The versatility of Lamb Ouzi allows for delightful regional variations and pairings.

**Variations:**
*   **Stuffing:** Some elaborate versions of Lamb Ouzi involve deboning a lamb shoulder, stuffing it with the spiced rice mixture, and then roasting it.
*   **Phyllo Pastry:** In some Gulf regions, a whole roasted lamb or a large cut might be encased in layers of crisp phyllo pastry before baking, creating a dramatic presentation and an extra textural element.
*   **Spice Blends:** While Seven Spice is common, other regions might lean more heavily on specific spices like cinnamon, cardamom, or even a hint of turmeric for a different flavor profile.
*   **Dried Fruits & Nuts:** Experiment with different dried fruits like apricots or prunes, and a wider variety of nuts such as cashews or walnuts in the rice.

**Pairings:**
*   **Beverages:** For a non-alcoholic option, a refreshing mint tea or a tangy jallab (date and grape molasses drink) complements the meal beautifully. For those who enjoy alcoholic beverages, a full-bodied red wine, such as a Cabernet Sauvignon or a Syrah, stands up well to the richness of the lamb.

## Frequently Asked Questions about Lamb Ouzi

**Q: What cut of lamb is best for Lamb Ouzi if I can't roast a whole lamb?**
A: A bone-in leg of lamb or a bone-in lamb shoulder is ideal. These cuts are large enough to feed a crowd, contain enough fat and connective tissue to become incredibly tender with slow roasting, and the bone adds significant flavor.

**Q: Can I make Lamb Ouzi ahead of time?**
A: Yes, partly. The lamb can be slow-roasted a day in advance. Once cooled, cover and refrigerate. Reheat gently in a low oven (covered) with a little broth until warmed through. The spiced rice is best made fresh, but you can prepare the base (sautéing onions and spices) ahead of time.

**Q: What if I don't have Seven Spice blend?**
A: You can create your own blend! A good substitute for the Lamb Ouzi would be a mix of equal parts ground allspice, black pepper, cinnamon, and a pinch of ground cloves, nutmeg, and cardamom. Cumin and coriander are also crucial and should be added separately as specified in the recipe.

**Q: How do I know when the lamb is perfectly tender?**
A: The lamb should be so tender that it nearly falls off the bone when gently prodded with a fork. An internal meat thermometer should read above 200°F (93°C) in the thickest part, indicating that the connective tissues have fully broken down.

Lamb Ouzi is more than just a recipe; it's a testament to the rich culinary heritage of the Middle East, a dish that transforms a simple meal into a memorable celebration. The commitment to slow roasting yields a lamb that is unbelievably tender and infused with aromatic spices, perfectly complemented by the fragrant spiced rice. Whether you're hosting a grand dinner or seeking to expand your culinary horizons, preparing this impressive Middle Eastern dinner recipe is a rewarding endeavor that will undoubtedly leave a lasting impression on your guests and your palate. Embrace the process, savor the aromas, and enjoy the glorious feast that is Lamb Ouzi.
